content: Are there any restriction-level lints enabled for clippy? (doesn't look like it) I'm noticing quite a few patch review items are about // SAFETY comments, thinking maybe enabling https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/#/undocumented_unsafe_blocks could help
Ah looks like it's listed here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/349 but just hasn't yet been enabled

Looks like they are configured here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/9be3b595987692933f6f0d9e235844f2f1077713/Makefile#L458. Based on the items in that list, it seems like these few from that list might be desired, if they're not enabled yet somewhere:

  • semicolon_outside_block (is in accordance with rustfmt)
  • undocumented_unsafe_blocks
  • unnecessary_safety_doc
  • unnecessary_safety_comment

After going to 1.69+ also no_mangle_with_rust_abi

I could send a patch to enable these if nobody else is working on it
There are probably quite a few things from pedantic that would also be useful. Some of that group can be pretty annoying but I wonder if enabling some portions would make sense

Please go ahead. Some of those may require some cleanups first, though. For the missing // SAFETY comments in particular, at some point we discussed writing a // SAFETY: TODO comment, in order to be able to enable the lint meanwhile even if those are not resolved, but ideally if one could add a proper // SAFETY comment while doing the cleanup, even better.

no_mangle_with_rust_abi

This one is done here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/1a31b93a64a91b58160b11600f1a03b48e09dde4 (for rust, I have to send the upgrade to mainline).

Another related topic we discussed long ago is splitting the -Dclippy::* categories into individual lints. This is fine for the moment since we pin the compiler for the moment, but even now it may make upgrades easier when new lints are added that we do not pass. Filled https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1023.

Ah looks like it's listed here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/349 but just hasn't yet been enabled

I have updated the list to include the PR links, the names they finally got and they version they were added (as we usually do).

(Thanks again to Lukas Wirth and Krishna Sundarram for implementing the ones we requested!).

I’m not sure if it is possible, but maybe it would be better to have a rust/clippy.toml, and point the makefile to it. Just so we can add more detailed comments and adjust lints without polluting Makefile
clippy.toml also lets you set a msrv so any newer lints don’t get enabled (maybe this can be done via CLI too?)

Trevor Gross said:

I’m not sure if it is possible, but maybe it would be better to have a rust/clippy.toml, and point the makefile to it. Just so we can add more detailed comments and adjust lints without polluting Makefile

Yeah, I think that can be a good idea (if we need the comments; otherwise I think the Makefile is OK and where flags are expected; though I guess they may be too many lints to list?), but I am not sure about the note here https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy#configure-the-behavior-of-some-lints:

clippy.toml or .clippy.toml cannot be used to allow/deny lints.

We could pass an @cfg argument, but that doesn't allow comments, but we can always handle them manually like we do in rust/bindgen_parameters.

Trevor Gross said:

clippy.toml also lets you set a msrv so any newer lints don’t get enabled (maybe this can be done via CLI too?)

Hmm... the docs say it is a config variable that some of the lints support, rather than something that disables all new lints. In other words, so that one does not use Rust features that did not exist in old compilers. Does it really disable new lints too? From a quick test, it doesn't seem to.

In any case, I am not sure about the CLI, the docs only mention the file and attribute.
Ah, I think I misunderstood clippy.toml and was thinking you could enable lints. And I don't think you can point clippy to a specific file. So nevermind, Makefile it is at least for now :smile:

Trevor Gross said:

And I don't think you can point clippy to a specific file.

No, but we could parse the file manually and build the flags to be passed (that is what we do with bindgen_parameters).
Would this be something you are interested in doing with this change?

I think it is mostly orthogonal, i.e. it can be done in separate patch series, unless we would have some useful comments to add already.

What kind of comments do you think we would have? I can think of:

  • Explaining cases that are not -D (e.g. -Wclippy::dbg_macro).
  • Explaining why something is not enabled even if we would like to (e.g. unnecessary_safety_comment).
  • Putting links to the docs for each flag (similar to https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/include/linux/compiler_attributes.h). In this case, however, Clippy's lints page pretty much removes the need.
And probably, if we want to do the split into a file, we may just want to include a new Makefile, e.g. scripts/Makefile.rust.
I actually did do this, created a PR here for review https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1025. If you prefer the makefile option, I can do that
That enables a lot more lints than just the unsafe comments, which of course can be pruned as needed (but they do all pass now)
@Benno Lossin it looks like you did most of the kernel/init things - I think I could use your help double checking the safety comments I added to those files on this PR

I think we should also document the ones in __internal.rs.
As for the undocumented unsafe blocks in macros.rs/init.rs, they do not really have a safety contract per-se, as they are internal API. So they should never be used outside of the module and it is a bit difficult to come up with the correct contract, since we do not have any types that we can give invariants. I will add some comments on github.

Is unsafe in those cases basically just a lint against accidentally using it somewhere? It seemed like it may be the case and I think I put comments like we are allowed to use this function, but it seems a bit weird to use unsafety as a lint if it can't actually cause UB. Perhaps better to use clippy::disallowed_types in the future if there winds up being a way to configure it?

Well misuse of some of these APIs can lead to UB, so I think they still are justified to be unsafe. Certain ones might never lead to UB, but it still would be strange to call those functions. But the disallowed_types lint does not work for the macro generated types, so I think this is fine.
Many of the internal APIs do not really have a contract, because it would be cumbersome to define it. But I guess it might be a good idea for completeness to define them regardless?

Well misuse of some of these APIs can lead to UB, so I think they still are justified to be unsafe

I wasn't sure about whether or not they could, so that does make sense.

Many of the internal APIs do not really have a contract, because it would be cumbersome to define it. But I guess it might be a good idea for completeness to define them regardless?

Cumbersome indeed, equally so to understand it. Especially since everything in __internal is pretty related. I'll defer to you for how in-depth to go, appreciate the review

A meta note, undocumented_unsafe_blocks has two configuration options accept-comment-above-statement and accept-comment-above-attributes. Mildly annoying that they are both false by default, but that is the reason some expression attributes had to get moved above the comments (since we have no way to configure these)

Trevor Gross said:

A meta note, undocumented_unsafe_blocks has two configuration options accept-comment-above-statement and accept-comment-above-attributes. Mildly annoying that they are both false by default, but that is the reason some expression attributes had to get moved above the comments (since we have no way to configure these)

Could you please copy an example of each? If we need to add .clippy.toml, that should not be a problem. But do we want them false anyway?

I believe that https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1025/files#diff-aa8f23b9e398ceb0f7cf3a26a4a7a81bdd4b9f015e3471c84c067880ac2fa8c9L169-R173 and https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1025/files#diff-aa8f23b9e398ceb0f7cf3a26a4a7a81bdd4b9f015e3471c84c067880ac2fa8c9R294-R298 are the two cases

I think it may be better to avoid .clippy.toml for now since that format is expected to change at some point. Or at least this seems like too minor of an annoyance to justify a new a new top-level file (but defer to you of course)

The second one looks a bit bad, especially the move of the #[allow(...)]. If we are going to do things that way, the CAST comment should also together with the SAFETY one -- did you split it that way for some reason?

Trevor Gross said:

I think it may be better to avoid .clippy.toml for now since that format is expected to change at some point.

In a backwards-incompatible way? That is quite surprising. Where is it documented? (I don't see it, and the main README.md talks about .clippy.toml).

Miguel Ojeda said:

The second one looks a bit bad, especially the move of the #[allow(...)]. If we are going to do things that way, the CAST comment should also together with the SAFETY one -- did you split it that way for some reason?

Just an aversion to attributes floating above comments, it's as close as possible without voiding the lint

Miguel Ojeda said:

Trevor Gross said:

I think it may be better to avoid .clippy.toml for now since that format is expected to change at some point.

In a backwards-incompatible way? That is quite surprising. Where is it documented? (I don't see it, and the main README.md talks about .clippy.toml).

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/3013 and https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/6625, I would just say the status seems "unknown" (I actually poked these earlier today). I have a very hard time imagining they'd totally remove clippy.toml, but the conversation is just a bit old and confusing

Thanks! #6625 is linked in our list, but that is not really about clippy.toml, no? i.e. clippy.toml is about configuring the lints, not enabling/disabling them. I don't think it is a duplicate (I asked about this back then in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/6798, and they closed it pointing to #6625, which I understood it meant clippy.toml would be only for configuring; or perhaps for nothing and everything is migrated to a new file).

In any case, it looks like they are still deciding what to do with a potential lints.toml file, but hopefully that does not mean they suddenly break clippy.toml after it has been documented in their main README.md...

By the way, this GH project has a few items: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/projects/3#column-12581089

Miguel Ojeda said:

In any case, it looks like they are still deciding what to do with a potential lints.toml file, but hopefully that does not mean they suddenly break clippy.toml after it has been documented in their main README.md...

I think that is my understanding too, I just got a little unsure after stumbling across that #3013
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/11162 I guess I might be able to make those options default for a future version, so it's probably ok as-is for now, without aclippy.toml config
